turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Number oh oh eleven eighty-seven, David R McKune versus Robert G Lile. Mr. McAllister. [Inaudible]

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. Choices have consequences, but they nonetheless remain choices. The mere withdrawal of prison privileges such as a personal television or how much an inmate may spend at the canteen, privileges which are not part of an inmate's sentence and to which an inmate has no legal entitlement, does not amount to constitutional compulsion in violation of the Fifth Amendment, certainly not when the reason for the withdrawal of those privileges is the inmate's failure to comply with an unquestionably legitimate treatment requirement that he accept responsibility for his offenses. [Inaudible]

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: The the problem, though, is he's he's forced, in effect, to to confront the the treatment possibility. It's not an option. I mean, i- t- the tough part of the case for me, I think, is the fact that this is this is not a scheme, as I understand the Federal scheme to be, in which the inmate says, I want to take advantage of this treatment program. This is a scheme in which the State says, you're going to take advantage of it, and if you don't take advantage of it, including the the the the admissions in the reports of other offenses, and so on, you're going to lose substantial privileges. That's that, to me, is the tough part of the case. [Inaudible]

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: That's true, Justice Souter, and our program does differ from the Federal program in that respect, but it is still a choice for Mr. Lile. He does not have to incriminate himself in any way. His refusal to participate is not at all incriminating. And what we're talking about in terms of what he may lose here are are really relatively mild incentives within the...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: that the participation required the prisoner to describe previous offenses...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that he may have been committe -- that he may have committed,

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: But what I'm saying is, if he simply refuses to participate, there's no incrimination whatsoever.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: what happens if he refuses? Is he put in a different type of confinement with different terms and conditions?

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: and you start at the same level of control.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Now, the prisoner says no, I don't want to participate in that program.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: The Kansas regulations say, if an inmate is is recommended for this treatment program and he refuses, he goes from level three to level one.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Is that where he is today?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, he's actually not, because he got an injunction against the in this case against...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: In the future , what's at issue is is not being deprived of benefits you already have, but of not giving you benefits that you don't have?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: chopped down from three to one was that the that he was grandfathered.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: he would simply not make it from one to two and from two to three, if he didn't go into the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Because there there's been some dispute between you and the other side as to whether this is simply the denial of a benefit or a punishment, and I'm not sure there's a whole lot of difference, but...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: people it looks more like a punishment.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Well then, the only person who's going to be in the position that Justice Scalia described is the person who's been getting in trouble along the way and never does progress.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: could the Kansas do that this with respect to a prisoner who's writing letters to the editor, to the newspaper, complaining about prison conditions? They say, well, this all this is privileged.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: I think that's a different case.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: the First Amendment different from the Self-Incrimination Clause? I mean, if in the one case you can't disadvantage the person for exercising that constitutional right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: here in in the terms of actually compelling him.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: But in the First Amendment context he has a right, a free speech right or a right of access to the courts that may be at issue.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: a- as as I recall it, the First Amendment says speaks of abridgement,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: right? And and the Fifth Amendment speaks of compulsion.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Compulsion, that's the language in the amendment.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: the State does have an interest in saying we want to rehabilitate you and it's best for you to confront your wrong.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: think it's potentially different, although it is potentially permissible constitutionally, but the question would become, what sort of a legitimate valid penological interest do we have? Do we have such an interest in having every inmate do that from the day they enter prison?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: This is very different. We could make that argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: confront the consequences of what they've done.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, it's much more than that in this case, because here the the the therapists are clear that denial is a big problem with sex offenders.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: only be meaningful if you compel them to admit to a cri- a new crime for which they could be...

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: with all due respect, Justice O'Connor, we would not say we're compelling them.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: penological interest satisfied in confining the program to those who want to be in it who will not be subject to this compulsion, if that's what it is? I mean, your your argument is that we have a penological interest, in effect, that justifies these consequences.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: fill your program without even having to raise the issue that involves these consequences, why do you have a penological interest in in the insistence that gives rise to this case?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Because the- these fellows have proven that most or, not most, but many of them will not voluntarily engage in this program,

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: who will, why why is there an interest in effect in forcing the issue for those who do not want to do it voluntarily?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Because we still have an interest in rehabilitating all of these sex offenders.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the State does not have an interest...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: the program i- is full, then i- i- i- is your interest in simply getting statements of of guilt or something from people who will never go into the program?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: No, and that's why he has the choice.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: that is not a voluntary program.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: If there's if there's as long as there's under Turner a legitimate penological interest, yes, potentially we could.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: legitimate reason to do it, potentially...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: because is there or isn't there? Is it in the case of every one that you incarcerate there is an interest in rehabilitating that person.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: And you have said that the first step in rehabilitation is acceptance of responsibility for the wrong that you've done.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: But you also rely, don't you, Mr. McAllister, on the fact that se- the therapists for this particular type of type of crime have come down very hard on the idea, and I take it perhaps there may not be the same body of support for that sort of treatment of other offenders.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: An- and there's certainly not, for example, treatment programs necessarily for burglars or robbers or other categories.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: But constitutionally, if if suddenly somebody got a good idea here and a a psychiatrist came forward saying yes, you you can you you can reform property criminals too, we'd be in exactly the same boat.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Yes, potentially, and and that's not, in our view, a constitutional problem, because this case this Court has long said for example none of the things we're talking about here are atypical in prison.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The the kind of conundrum that I that that puzzles me that I don't have an answer to is is illustrated by the trial process itself.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, Justice Breyer, to say the first is different, or or in essence we're different, because all of our this takes place inside the prison.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: are saying you can have two classes of prisoners, those who have confessed to their crimes and those who haven't.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Except, Justice Kennedy it may do that.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: conceded that a prosecutor, say, in the sexual history says, I committed X, Y, Z rapes and that a prosecutor, as long as there's no statute of limitations problem, in Kansas unlike, as I understand the federal program is, a prosecutor could say, okay, now we're going to indict you for that.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: But my understanding is they could do that in the federal program as well, because there's no immunity granted under the Federal program either.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: they don't suffer any loss of anything if they if they don't make the statement and...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: under yours, in any case, whether the Feds do it or not, under yours the prosecutor could use that information, couldn't he?

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: May I ask you, Mr. McAllister, do you know i- if and there's similar programs in a lot of states, as I understand it.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: E- Justice Stevens, it's my understanding that some may, although I don't know the exact number, and I do know from the state amicus brief the eighteen states that signed onto that obviously think immunity is a bad idea and, as I said, the Federal Government does not immunize the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: inmates who participate in the program. Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: And o- of those, how many are like Kansas? That is, it isn't a voluntary?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, I don't know the the answer to that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: [NS] Mr. Garre, why does the Federal Government not think it a good idea to grant immunity?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Justice Scalia, the Federal Government has has a very limited program it applies in only one facility nation-wide.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: did the Federal Government think it not a good idea to grant immunity in its program?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Oh, the the Federal Government reserves the right of the same confidentiality limits that the State does.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Only those things can be prosecuted? I thought I thought that it that i- that that prosecution was available for anything that was that was disclosed, although although there was confidentiality.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Federal Government has has not given use immunity u- for...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: and that's absolutely clear from the from the waiver the confidentiality statement that that inmates sign before they enroll in the program.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Government re- deprive the prisoner of any benefits or programs if he refuses to e- engage in the program? The the Federal Government doesn't apply the same in- incentive schemes that the State of...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: the the o- the overriding incentive is, of course, the value of the treatment that the inmate receives.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: not the nothing is threatened or carried out in the Federal program to deprive the nonconsenting prisoner of any privilege,

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: th- tha- that's true up front in terms of the incentive scheme.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: the choice to go into the program is strictly the inmates.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's the way the Federal Bureau of...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: It is at a more desirable...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: doesn't he isn't he doesn't get out of the rotten place he's in to to a better...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: a benefit that he currently has?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, we we don't we think think that the that the privileges that we're talking about in the case of pra- Kansas, TV ownership, personal TV in the cell, visitation privileges beyond immediate family, and and lawyers, canteen expenditures, these aren't these aren't...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: the right to take other programs in the prison?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The th- the privileges are reduced.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Substantial reduction in how much you can earn in prison, what jobs you can do in prison, isn't that so?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, e- th- there's there is a definite reduction, and the flip side of that is Kansas reserves the higher privileges, the more modern facilities to those inmates who choose to take the constructive steps towards reentering society.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Okay, but there's no there's no reduction...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: in the Federal system, is that correct?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: There's no the the the Federal system currently doesn't employ the same earnable privilege scheme that the Kansas person...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's correct, except that the inmate can be sent back to his parent...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: have said you said in your opening statement, and you seem to be backing away from it, you said the Federal program is a voluntary program.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Now that's  the issue here, isn't it?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: with respect, we think that that is the issue.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, I I th- foremost because the Fifth Amendment says, compelled self-incrimination.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: of c- of compulsion does does not meet it? I that is that is to say, you can have two classes of inmates, those who've confessed and those who haven't, for all of prison life? Well And and isn't there a danger, then, of of of of of inducing innocent people to to confess?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: I think that type of hypotheticals is much different, much further afield than the...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: say inducing, does it? It says compelling.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: we in fact have two classes in in in all prison systems? Those who have pleaded guilty and and have gotten a relatively short sentence by reason of their guilty plea for a particular crime, and those who have refused to plead guilty and have gotten a longer sentence because of their refusal to do so.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: not just not being able to spend as much at the PX, but but they're there for another another fifteen years.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: anything other than physical, or psychological?

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: I mean, in in outside prison we know, at least this Court's precedent has said losing your membership in the bar, losing your job, that counts as compulsion, even though no one is putting you on the rack and screw.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: And and and we think we agree with Judge Friendly and others who have suggested that those cases lie at the outer reaches of this Court's Fifth Amendment jurisprudence, a- and we think that the denial of the sorts of common, routine privileges at issue in this case, TV privileges, canteen expenditures, don't even come...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: loss of visiting privileges? That could be crucial to a prisoner.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, it's not a complete loss in this case.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, I I I mean, the further the the Court goes out in that direction, then obviously at some point that that program would be more difficult to defend under the Turner...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: no limit on what they can do here.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, w- with respect, we we don't think that that's the Kansas program.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: i- e- again, m- I would disagree with the d- the the characterization of the Kansas program, but but more importantly we think that the judgment made by the Federal Bureau of Prison and the judgment made by the Kansas prison officials are well within the range of decisions that this Court's prison decisions...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Y- Your description presents a different situation.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court If you're a prisoner in Kansas and you commit a rape while in prison, you get the same penalties that Mr. Lile gets.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: you mean, i- someone who commits arson in prison gets only those penalties? He isn't prosecuted for committing arson? There could be a potential prosection if they...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: number of years in more in jail.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: But under the State system they get moved down to the same level that Mr. Lile is, and in fact their punishment could be worse,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the arsonist considers that the least of his worries.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: punishment, you assume your answer in your favor.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: I think the difference, Your Honor, is is somewhat illustrated by the Government's attorney in that this is not voluntary, and that once you achieve a level the State has state has set up a structure, Your Honor, under which if you're good, you do your job, you get to a certain level, and that's for everybody.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Is it true that their system is, you will be awarded privileges as long as you participate in treatment, but our privileges are open only to people who participate in treatment?

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Because people in the prison system who do not participate in treatment get the same privileges.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Why did he lose his privileges? Well, he hasn't, technically, Your Honor, lost his...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: have no ability to go into the program?

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Have no ability, but but other there w-

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: could be other sex offenders in the prison who are not required to take SATP, for example, if there was a if if for some reason there was a statutory rape penalty they may not be required to take SATP.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Court, what cases do you think most most strongly support your position?

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Your Honor, I think the cases of Garrity, Gardner, Cunningham support the...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: talking about i- loss of job an in a in a in a civilian society.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Specifically as to the job, Mr. Lile, if if any inmate in the prison system in Kansas, if they were, for example, in minimum security can work an outside job.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: get to work a job that is even equivalent to that.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: You're talking about a situation where in civilian life the person who le- loses his job loses his livelihood, basically.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: He will eat, Your Honor, but at the same same same time a policeman on guard or a policeman in Garrity could go out and get another job and earn something, an equivalent wage, or possibly even a better wage.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But that's you know, i- either you do it, or you're going to get life.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: I don't Your Honor, our view is not is that that is not compulsion, because what is being extended to the murder suspect is a benefit, some way to improve your lot.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I don't think that would be...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: them in start them off in the worst situation and just say,

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: certainly doesn't benefit prisoners as a class.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: surely can't turn on that on on that, whether you characterize it as giving them a benefit or depriving them of one a benefit they all...

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Well, the Constitution obviously prohibits any kind of sanction for the invocation of your Fifth Amendment rights.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: you said it in your brief, and I wanted to make sure that it really is your position.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: and you never get out of it unless you take this program?

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Your Honor, I I do think that's certainly closer to the const- that that would be closer to a constitutional law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: be. That's what you said in your brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: So the whole thing, then, it comes down to, subtraction is no good.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: If they could set up a system or a structure or or fashion r- some rules obviously, they don't have that now, then I don't think that would be unconstitutional.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So so you think the the sentencing guidelines would be invalid if, instead of the current provision, which gives you good points for acceptance of responsibility, it rather gave you bad points for refusing to accept responsibility.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: I d- I do believe so,

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: if that's if that's so, does this case as my understanding of it is that the prison created a new policy, and that policy was that everybody was at level one unless you participate in a recommended program, that that was their new policy, but that your client was grandfathered in at level three because he was in prison at the time.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Because all inmates in the pri- there were th- there are not just inmates at level three who were grandfathered in.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: changed the rule and said, if you don't participate in a recommended program, you can't get to level three.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: If they have not they have not changed the rule.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: this is a a product that the they don't have the facility to give this course to everyone, so they say, when you're getting closer to release time, you get it.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Well, the State wants als- wants their sex offenders to take it.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: their first year, I when what I understand.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But your brief described a system where, suppose we had all sex offenders, they go in at level one unless they take the program.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: That's something that doesn't exist in current world only because of a lack of resources, that Kansas can't give this program to all the people who would qualify, so it concentrates on the people who have served a substantial part of their sentence already.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: That is correct, and and and kee- please understand, Your Honor, that that is only one possible solution that would allow the State to continue to run its SATP.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Your Honor, I agree with that.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: solution that isn't consistent with the therapist's idea that this is how it should be done.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: sure why it has to be a written statement where you f- you fess everything up.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: know I asked your opponent this question the extent to which other States have granted immunity to solve this problem?

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: There are a couple of States that I know off-hand, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: programs are important, I take it, r- really important, and and they're thinking that this is a very important way to run them, I I give them that.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Well, first, Your Honor, if this Court doesn't want to get drawn into a benefit versus a punishment distinction, you don't have to follow that for this rule.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: answer the question, though, and I have the same problem Justice Breyer does.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Well, I d- I do believe that I do believe that that sanction or or what you can look at is, you could look at the thi- this Court's ruling as to what is a voluntary statement in the Collin case, whether or not he's being able to make an unconstrained choice.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to what Sandin looked to in in true, not in the context of the Fifth Amendment, but in another context, and that is whether what you have been deprived of in prison is is beyond what is the normal expectation of prison life.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Your Honor, I think that does a couple of things, and and why we disagree with Sandin.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Amendment rule in the prison system...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: we don't have a rule that arrives that that applies in the Fifth Amendment arena with regard to depriving people of of things to which they are not entitled as free citizens.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: it seems to me we need a new rule for in-prison cases.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Your Honor, I would agree to you that Mr. Lile has no liberty interest at play here, but I would also suggest that there is no liberty interest in an at-will employment.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Don't call it liberty, just call it a right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: he's entitled as a free citizen to have that.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: But but the but the way that the State has set up its structure, they have made rules that they want everybody working.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Okay so we'll add to it, there has to be a and and the the State of Kansas is perfectly willing to add to it, it has to be for a legitimate penological purpose.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: And certainly I would I would agree with what's previously said, that there could be a legitimately penological purpose in confessing, in coming clean, and that you will not be a model prisoner unless you take responsibility for your crime, whether it be a sex offense or whether it be a murder.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I was thinking about that, and the trouble with analogies, if you get one that's very close, you become uncertain again.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Your Honor, I I I agree with I agree with with the concern over the technical distinction between benefit and penalty.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: It's not the grandfather I mean, i- it it is the grandfather, isn't it, in his case that makes him well you said there were some other things, and I'd like to be sure to have them in mind, that make it a penalty and not just the withholding of a privilege.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Assume for the moment Mr. Lile were not grandfathered in.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: Well he would enter the prison system at at intake level one.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: eighteen years from now the State will ask him to take sex offender treatment.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: He will get all the way .

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: two thousand nineteen they say to him, please take...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, one one difference, certainly between the First Amendment and its cases and the Fifth Amendment is that there is a compulsion requirement in order to invoke the Fifth Amendment, where where the First Amendment doesn't have anything like that.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: That's what distinguishes the detriment and the benefit.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The Kansas program would be perfectly okay, in in in your estimation, if it provided that at the end of eighteen years of twenty-year sentences, or two years before the end of their sentence, all sex offenders shall be reduced, all sex offenders shall be reduced to prison level one.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Why not, because then a- then if they choose to come in this program they will be getting the benefit of going back up to three.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: think that would be constitutional, Your Honor.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: I think it would be set up as a way to get around SATP and a way to get around the invocation of your Fifth...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Amendment rights. It would be an artifice.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, of course it's an artifice.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: the one the one thing I would encourage this Court to look at is, look at how it affects itself on the prisoner.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It's the same it's the same in my hypothetical, though.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: If it's entirely divorced from the program or the Fifth Amendment, then Your Honor, then that potentially would be constitutional.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: to decide what is compulsion you've got to look at i- how other people are treated, in effect.

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: And I agree, if the State had set up a...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: let me ask you to concentrate on that question a little harder, because you did say in your brief that if all sex offenders not all prisoners, all sex offenders on day one were in put in class one, they could stay there and never get out unless the the carrot that was dangled was taken.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: objecting to a distinction between classes of...

turns.name: MATHEW J. WILTANGER
turns.text: No, Your Honor, you're you're correct, a- and again that's consistent with the view that we took between the benefit and the sanction,

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: a a a a a comparative analysis as as between classes of prisoners depending on their offense?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Now, I I I understood you the other way.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: saying with the cohort of prisoners you'll go along for ten years, all of them earning points and credits.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Yes, but I mean, in our view there's an important penological reason for doing that.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: But why wouldn't the same penological reason justify taking away all privileges? In other words, why did you make the concession you made at the beginning of your rebuttal?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Because, all I'm saying is at some point even even Sandin recognizes at some point things are atypical and they exceed the line, so although he could be moved to disciplinary segregation without a due process hearing, if he was put in solitary confinement, that might have been different.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: solitary confinement it would be justifiable, if at all, because of a penological reason, the way he had behaved in prison, creating dangerous conditions, et cetera.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Because the text of the Fifth Amendment says no person shall be compelled, and the question is compulsion, and at some point, if we took away everything, or we made him work twenty hours a day or we could do things to him that I think the Court would would have...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: your argument, you're saying, one reason why you should not characterize this as compulsion is the valid penological reason for doing it.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: suggestion is, if there if if if if the State should say, look, the protection of these victims on the outside, who are going to be preyed upon by this person if not rehabilitated, is just as important as preventing people from setting fires in their cells, and therefore, if we take away all privileges to the fire-setters, we've got an equally good penological reason to take away all privileges from the person who won't go into the program.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: And that that I agree with.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: all the privileges, because it has to rise to the level of compulsion.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: extend your time for two minutes, Mr. McAllister, because you really didn't have a chance to say much of anything, I'm afraid.

turns.name: THE MARSHAL
turns.text: The honorable court is now adjourned until Monday next at ten o'clock.
